quiz.question: Organ transplantation saves thousands of lives each year. What is transplantation?
options: The transfer of an organ or tissue from one body to another
A surgical procedure that replaced a failing organ with a healthy one
A medical advance that required both technical skill and donor availability
A form of treatment that extended life beyond what other interventions could
quiz.answer: The transfer of an organ or tissue from one body to another
quiz.explanation:
Transplantation is the transfer of tissue or organs — from donor to recipient.
• transplantation = the transfer of tissue or organs to another body or location
• From Latin: 'transplantare' — to replant
• 'Heart transplantation', 'plant transplantation' — moving from one place to another

quiz.question: The augmentation of staff numbers allowed the department to reduce waiting times. What is augmentation?
options: An increase or addition — the action of making something larger
A staffing measure that addressed an identifiable capacity problem
An investment that produced measurable improvements in service
A change that required approval from senior management
quiz.answer: An increase or addition — the action of making something larger
quiz.explanation:
Augmentation is the act of increasing — enlarging or supplementing.
• augmentation = the act of making something larger or supplementing it
• From Latin: 'augere' — to increase
• 'Workforce augmentation', 'breast augmentation' — increase or addition

quiz.question: Fermentation converts sugar into alcohol — a process central to brewing. What is fermentation?
options: A chemical process in which microorganisms convert substances into simpler ones
The biological mechanism by which yeast converts sugars to alcohol
A natural process exploited by humans for thousands of years
A reaction that required the right temperature and yeast to proceed, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A chemical process in which microorganisms convert substances into simpler ones
quiz.explanation:
Fermentation is microbial conversion — organisms breaking down sugars.
• fermentation = the chemical breakdown of substances by microorganisms
• From Latin: 'fermentare' — to leaven
• 'Fermentation of grapes', 'bread fermentation' — microbial conversion

quiz.question: Her presentation of the data was clear and the committee approved the proposal unanimously. What is a presentation?
options: The way something is shown or set out — an act of formal display
A communication prepared to persuade a specific audience
A prepared performance of data or argument to a decision-making body
A structured delivery designed to achieve a defined outcome
quiz.answer: The way something is shown or set out — an act of formal display
quiz.explanation:
A presentation is a formal display — the way something is shown or communicated.
• presentation = the act of presenting; the manner of display
• From Latin: 'praesentare' — to place before
• 'Business presentation', 'presentation of evidence' — formal showing

quiz.question: The advertisement was withdrawn after complaints about misrepresentation. What is misrepresentation?
options: A false or misleading description — giving an incorrect account
A form of advertising that gave a false impression of the product
A legal wrong that entitled the injured party to seek remedies
A deceptive claim that created expectations the product could not meet
quiz.answer: A false or misleading description — giving an incorrect account
quiz.explanation:
Misrepresentation is a false account — presenting something incorrectly.
• misrepresentation = a false or misleading account
• From 'mis-' + 'representation'
• 'Fraudulent misrepresentation', 'misrepresent the facts' — false description

quiz.question: The billionaire's ostentation — the private jets, the multiple mansions — was impossible to miss. What is ostentation?
options: A showy display of wealth or luxury — pretentious exhibitionism
The deliberate communication of wealth through visible consumption
A lifestyle that valued display over privacy
The use of possessions to establish and signal status
quiz.answer: A showy display of wealth or luxury — pretentious exhibitionism
quiz.explanation:
Ostentation is a vulgar display — exhibiting wealth or ability to impress others.
• ostentation = a pretentious display of wealth or ability
• From Latin: 'ostentare' — to display
• 'Without ostentation', 'ostentatious wealth' — showy, conspicuous display

quiz.question: The word 'home' carries connotations of warmth and safety that 'house' does not. What is a connotation?
options: An idea or feeling that a word implies beyond its literal meaning
The emotional resonance that distinguishes two otherwise similar words
The cultural associations that accumulate around a word over time
The layer of meaning that is felt but not formally defined
quiz.answer: An idea or feeling that a word implies beyond its literal meaning
quiz.explanation:
A connotation is an implied meaning — the associations a word carries beyond its definition.
• connotation = an idea or feeling evoked by a word beyond its denotation
• From Latin: 'connotare' — to mark alongside
• 'Negative connotations', 'connotations of luxury' — implied associations

quiz.question: After the ceremony, the guests engaged in hearty potation. What is potation?
options: The act of drinking — especially a draught of alcohol
The communal drinking that marked the conclusion of a formal event
The portion of an evening devoted to informal socialising over drinks
The alcoholic component of a celebration that followed the formal part
quiz.answer: The act of drinking — especially a draught of alcohol
quiz.explanation:
Potation is the act of drinking — a draught, especially of alcohol.
• potation = a drink; the act of drinking
• From Latin: 'potare' — to drink
• 'Hearty potation', 'potations' — drinks or the act of drinking

quiz.question: The smell of fresh bread was a temptation she found hard to resist. What is temptation?
options: The desire to do something appealing but potentially unwise
The pull of something attractive that she was trying to avoid
A stimulus strong enough to overcome an otherwise firm resolve
An urge produced by a specific sensory experience
quiz.answer: The desire to do something appealing but potentially unwise
quiz.explanation:
Temptation is enticement — the desire to do something one might not otherwise do.
• temptation = the desire to do something that may be unwise or wrong
• From Latin: 'temptare' — to test
• 'Resist temptation', 'the temptation to quit' — the pull toward something

quiz.question: She spent two years writing her doctoral dissertation. What is a dissertation?
options: A long formal essay — especially one submitted for an academic degree
A piece of sustained original research completed for a doctorate
The principal work through which a candidate demonstrated their scholarship
A formal piece of writing that constituted the final stage of doctoral study
quiz.answer: A long formal essay — especially one submitted for an academic degree
quiz.explanation:
A dissertation is a long formal essay — typically for a higher degree.
• dissertation = a long essay on a particular subject
• From Latin: 'dissertare' — to discuss
• 'Doctoral dissertation', 'write a dissertation' — extended formal essay

quiz.question: The deportation of refugees drew international condemnation. What is deportation?
options: The forced removal of a person from a country
The exercise of legal authority to remove those without the right to remain
A government action opposed by human rights organisations
The transfer of individuals to their country of origin by state authorities
quiz.answer: The forced removal of a person from a country
quiz.explanation:
Deportation is forced removal — expelling a person from a country by authority.
• deportation = the expulsion of a person from a country
• From Latin: 'deportare' — to carry away
• 'Face deportation', 'deportation order' — forced removal

quiz.question: The protests were a manifestation of deep public anger. What is a manifestation?
options: An outward sign or expression of something — a visible form taken
A public demonstration of feelings that had been building for some time
A development that gave political leaders clear evidence of public feeling
An event that communicated what surveys had been indicating
quiz.answer: An outward sign or expression of something — a visible form taken
quiz.explanation:
A manifestation is a visible expression — something made apparent through signs.
• manifestation = an outward sign of something; a visible expression
• From Latin: 'manifestare' — to make evident
• 'A manifestation of grief', 'manifest anxiety' — a visible sign

quiz.question: Deforestation in the Amazon threatens both biodiversity and the global climate. What is deforestation?
options: The large-scale removal of forest — clearing trees from an area
The conversion of forest to farmland or other uses
A process that reduced tree cover below ecologically sustainable levels
The destruction of forest habitats for agricultural or commercial purposes
quiz.answer: The large-scale removal of forest — clearing trees from an area
quiz.explanation:
Deforestation is the clearing of forests — removing trees on a large scale.
• deforestation = the clearing of forest cover from land
• From 'de-' + 'forest'
• 'Tropical deforestation', 'halt deforestation' — removing forest cover

quiz.question: Wine critics rely on gustation as much as on smell to evaluate a wine. What is gustation?
options: The sense of taste — the ability to perceive flavour
The act of tasting, especially with discernment
The physical mechanism by which flavour is perceived, as the evidence showed
The sensory evaluation performed through the palate
quiz.answer: The sense of taste — the ability to perceive flavour
quiz.explanation:
Gustation is the sense of taste — the perception of flavour.
• gustation = the act or faculty of tasting
• From Latin: 'gustare' — to taste
• 'Gustatory experience', 'gustation and olfaction' — the sense of taste

quiz.question: The pipe was blocked by a hard incrustation of limescale. What is incrustation?
options: A hard coating or crust that forms on a surface
The deposit of minerals that accumulated inside a pipe over time
A solid layer formed by precipitation from a liquid
A build-up that required chemical treatment to remove
quiz.answer: A hard coating or crust that forms on a surface
quiz.explanation:
An incrustation is a hard crust — a deposit that forms on a surface.
• incrustation = a hard coating or crust formed on a surface
• From Latin: 'incrustare' — to cover with a crust
• 'Limescale incrustation', 'mineral incrustation' — a hard surface crust

quiz.question: He greeted her with an elaborate salutation that made her laugh. What is a salutation?
options: A greeting — a gesture or phrase used to welcome someone
A formal acknowledgement of another person's arrival
A social ritual whose elaborateness reflected the relationship
A conventional opening that marked the beginning of an encounter
quiz.answer: A greeting — a gesture or phrase used to welcome someone
quiz.explanation:
A salutation is a greeting — an act or phrase used to acknowledge another.
• salutation = a greeting
• From Latin: 'salutare' — to greet
• 'Opening salutation', 'a salutation to the crowd' — a formal greeting

quiz.question: She had built a reputation for honest dealing over twenty years. What is reputation?
options: The opinion generally held of someone — their standing in others' eyes
The accumulated result of consistent behaviour over a long period, as the evidence showed
A form of social capital that opened doors others found closed
The public image that her behaviour had created and maintained
quiz.answer: The opinion generally held of someone — their standing in others' eyes
quiz.explanation:
Reputation is what others think — the general opinion held of someone's character.
• reputation = the general opinion held of someone
• From Latin: 'reputare' — to think over
• 'Damage a reputation', 'by reputation' — what people generally think

quiz.question: The imputation of dishonesty was unjust and deeply offensive to her. What is imputation?
options: The attribution of something negative — an accusation by inference
A suggestion strong enough to cause damage without stating anything directly
An inference made by those who chose to read the facts negatively
A charge too vague to answer but too damaging to ignore
quiz.answer: The attribution of something negative — an accusation by inference
quiz.explanation:
Imputation is attribution — the ascribing of fault or blame to someone.
• imputation = the attribution of fault or responsibility to someone
• From Latin: 'imputare' — to reckon
• 'An imputation of guilt', 'impute bad motives' — attributing blame

quiz.question: A thorough evaluation of the programme was completed before further funding was approved. What is evaluation?
options: A formal assessment of something's value or effectiveness
A systematic review that gathered evidence before a funding decision
A process designed to determine whether objectives had been met
A professional exercise that used agreed criteria to reach a judgement
quiz.answer: A formal assessment of something's value or effectiveness
quiz.explanation:
Evaluation is formal assessment — measuring effectiveness against criteria.
• evaluation = the making of a judgement about quality or value
• From Latin: 'evaluare' — to value
• 'Performance evaluation', 'evaluate the evidence' — formal assessment

quiz.question: The continuation of the project depended on securing further funding. What is continuation?
options: The carrying on of something — the action of continuing
The condition for the project remaining active beyond its initial phase
The state of a project that depended on decisions not yet made
The extension of an activity past its originally planned endpoint
quiz.answer: The carrying on of something — the action of continuing
quiz.explanation:
Continuation is the action of continuing — carrying on without stopping.
• continuation = the action of carrying on or being carried on
• From Latin: 'continuare' — to join together
• 'A continuation of yesterday's meeting', 'ensure continuation' — keeping going

quiz.question: In any equation, both sides must balance. What is an equation?
options: A mathematical statement that two expressions are equal
A formal expression of the relationship between quantities
A mathematical tool that describes how variables relate
A formula in which values on both sides are equivalent
quiz.answer: A mathematical statement that two expressions are equal
quiz.explanation:
An equation is a mathematical balance — stating that two expressions are equal.
• equation = a mathematical statement of equality; a situation of balance
• From Latin: 'aequare' — to make equal
• 'Solve an equation', 'factor into the equation' — mathematical or general balance

quiz.question: What he called love was dismissed by his friends as mere infatuation. What is infatuation?
options: An intense but short-lived passion — irrational obsessive attachment
A form of emotion more powerful in the short term than genuine love, as the evidence showed
A state that his friends recognised as temporary even if he did not
A feeling strong enough to impair his judgment and perspective
quiz.answer: An intense but short-lived passion — irrational obsessive attachment
quiz.explanation:
Infatuation is irrational obsessive passion — intense but usually brief.
• infatuation = an intense short-lived passion
• From Latin: 'infatuare' — to make a fool of
• 'Mere infatuation', 'infatuated with' — foolish, intense attraction

quiz.question: The derivation of the word tells you its origin and original meaning. What is derivation?
options: The tracing of a word's origin — its etymology
The historical process by which a word acquired its current form
The linguistic analysis that connected a word to its source
The information that revealed which language a word came from
quiz.answer: The tracing of a word's origin — its etymology
quiz.explanation:
Derivation is origin — the tracing of a word's history and source.
• derivation = the origin of a word; the action of deriving
• From Latin: 'derivare' — to lead away
• 'The derivation of a term', 'derive from Latin' — linguistic origin

quiz.question: The soldiers endured months of privation in the freezing mountains. What is privation?
options: The lack of basic necessities — hardship from being deprived
The physical condition that resulted from a lack of food and warmth
The suffering that accumulated when supply lines broke down
An experience that tested endurance beyond what training had prepared
quiz.answer: The lack of basic necessities — hardship from being deprived
quiz.explanation:
Privation is hardship through lack — suffering from the absence of necessities.
• privation = the lack of basic necessities
• From Latin: 'privare' — to deprive
• 'Years of privation', 'endure privation' — going without necessities

quiz.question: Sleep deprivation seriously impairs cognitive function. What is deprivation?
options: The harmful lack of something necessary — being deprived of something essential
The condition produced by removing what the body or mind requires, as the evidence showed
A deficit measurable through its effects on performance
An absence of the thing that normal functioning depended upon
quiz.answer: The harmful lack of something necessary — being deprived of something essential
quiz.explanation:
Deprivation is harmful lack — the damaging absence of something essential.
• deprivation = the lack of something considered necessary
• From Latin: 'deprivare' — to strip away
• 'Sleep deprivation', 'social deprivation' — harmful absence of something needed

quiz.question: The conductor received a standing ovation at the end of the concert. What is an ovation?
options: A sustained burst of enthusiastic applause
A form of audience appreciation reserved for exceptional performances
The spontaneous response of an audience to a performance
The highest form of appreciation available to a concert audience
quiz.answer: A sustained burst of enthusiastic applause
quiz.explanation:
An ovation is enthusiastic applause — sustained approval from an audience.
• ovation = enthusiastic applause; a standing ovation includes rising to one's feet
• From Latin: 'ovare' — to rejoice
• 'Standing ovation', 'receive an ovation' — sustained enthusiastic applause

quiz.question: The company prided itself on innovation — constantly developing new products. What is innovation?
options: The introduction of something new — an original development
A competitive advantage sustained through continuous product development
A culture that welcomed new ideas rather than defending existing ones
The organisational capacity that kept the company ahead of competitors
quiz.answer: The introduction of something new — an original development
quiz.explanation:
Innovation is introducing something new — original creation or development.
• innovation = a new method, idea, or product
• From Latin: 'innovare' — to renew
• 'Technological innovation', 'culture of innovation' — introducing the new

quiz.question: The relaxation of border controls allowed freer movement. What is relaxation?
options: The loosening of rules or tension — a reduction in strictness
A policy change that reduced the controls previously in place
An adjustment that reflected a changed assessment of the risks
A modification to the rules that required parliamentary approval
quiz.answer: The loosening of rules or tension — a reduction in strictness
quiz.explanation:
Relaxation is a loosening — a reduction in tension or strictness.
• relaxation = the lessening of restriction or tension; rest
• From Latin: 'relaxare' — to loosen
• 'Relaxation of rules', 'relax and unwind' — loosening or resting

quiz.question: The annexation of the territory was condemned as a violation of international law. What is annexation?
options: The taking of territory — incorporating land into another state by force
An act by which one country claimed sovereignty over another's land
A unilateral act opposed by the international community
The forcible incorporation of land without the consent of its inhabitants
quiz.answer: The taking of territory — incorporating land into another state by force
quiz.explanation:
Annexation is the taking of territory — absorbing land into a state.
• annexation = the act of annexing territory
• From Latin: 'annexare' — to join
• 'Illegal annexation', 'territorial annexation' — absorbing another's land

quiz.question: His persistent lateness was a constant source of vexation to his team. What is vexation?
options: Annoyance and frustration — the state of being vexed
The irritation produced by behaviour that continued despite requests to change
A level of frustration that was beginning to affect working relationships
An emotional response to repeated failure to meet basic professional standards
quiz.answer: Annoyance and frustration — the state of being vexed
quiz.explanation:
Vexation is annoyance — the feeling of being frustrated or irritated.
• vexation = the state of being annoyed or frustrated
• From Latin: 'vexare' — to shake, trouble
• 'A source of vexation', 'vexing problem' — annoyance and frustration

quiz.question: The centralization of decision-making frustrated regional managers. What is centralization?
options: The concentration of control in a single authority or location
A management choice that moved authority away from regional offices
An organisational restructuring that created a single point of decision
A policy that reduced the autonomy of those at the edges of the structure
quiz.answer: The concentration of control in a single authority or location
quiz.explanation:
Centralization is concentration of control — gathering authority into one place.
• centralization = the concentration of control at a single point
• From 'centre' + '-ize' + '-ation'
• 'Centralise power', 'decentralization' — gathering control to the centre

quiz.question: The optimization of the supply chain reduced costs by fifteen percent. What is optimization?
options: The action of making the best possible use of something
A process of improvement that identified and eliminated inefficiencies
A technical exercise that applied analysis to a practical problem
A formal review that identified where performance could be improved
quiz.answer: The action of making the best possible use of something
quiz.explanation:
Optimization is finding the best outcome — making something as effective as possible.
• optimization = making the best use of a situation or resource
• From Latin: 'optimus' — best
• 'Route optimization', 'performance optimization' — making as good as possible

quiz.question: The characterization of the villain was the novel's greatest strength. What is characterization?
options: The creation and portrayal of fictional characters — or the description of someone
The craft through which fictional personalities were made believable
The skill that separated a great novel from a merely competent one
The literary technique that determined whether readers felt the characters were real
quiz.answer: The creation and portrayal of fictional characters — or the description of someone
quiz.explanation:
Characterization is the portrayal of character — creating believable personalities.
• characterization = the way characters are created and described
• From Greek: 'kharakter' — a distinctive mark
• 'Vivid characterization', 'characterize as dangerous' — portraying character

quiz.question: The transaction required authorization from two senior managers. What is authorization?
options: Official permission — the granting of authority to do something
A formal approval that allowed a process to proceed
A control mechanism requiring sign-off at an appropriate level
A governance requirement applicable to transactions above a threshold
quiz.answer: Official permission — the granting of authority to do something
quiz.explanation:
Authorization is official permission — the sanctioning of an action.
• authorization = the action of authorizing
• From Latin: 'auctor' — originator
• 'Require authorization', 'authorization code' — formal permission

quiz.question: The intense heat caused vaporization of the liquid in seconds. What is vaporization?
options: The conversion of a substance into vapour — turning liquid into gas
The phase change from liquid to gaseous state
The result of applying heat to a liquid beyond its boiling point, as the evidence showed
The process by which intense heat transformed the liquid state
quiz.answer: The conversion of a substance into vapour — turning liquid into gas
quiz.explanation:
Vaporization is conversion to vapour — the phase change from liquid to gas.
• vaporization = the process of converting a liquid or solid into vapour
• From Latin: 'vapor' — steam
• 'Flash vaporization', 'vaporize the fuel' — turning to gas

quiz.question: Sensitization training helped staff recognise signs of abuse. What is sensitization?
options: The process of making aware or responsive — heightening sensitivity
Training that changed how people perceived and interpreted situations
A programme that increased staff awareness of a specific issue
An educational intervention that reduced the chance of missed signs
quiz.answer: The process of making aware or responsive — heightening sensitivity
quiz.explanation:
Sensitization is increasing awareness or responsiveness — making more sensitive.
• sensitization = the process of making responsive to certain stimuli
• From 'sensitive' + '-ize' + '-ation'
• 'Allergen sensitization', 'cultural sensitization' — increasing sensitivity

quiz.question: The ruling party was divided into factions that rarely agreed. What is a faction?
options: A small group within a larger one — often in opposition to leadership
A set of politicians who shared a common position or ideology
A division within a party that made unified action difficult
A group that placed internal loyalty above loyalty to the whole, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A small group within a larger one — often in opposition to leadership
quiz.explanation:
A faction is an internal group — a subset of an organisation often in opposition.
• faction = a small group within a larger one, especially one causing dissension
• From Latin: 'factio' — a making or party
• 'Rival factions', 'factional dispute' — dissenting subgroup

quiz.question: Sound travels as alternating regions of compression and rarefaction through the air. What is rarefaction?
options: A reduction in density — the thinning out of a medium
The region of lower pressure in a sound wave
The opposite of compression in the propagation of a wave
A physical state produced by the expansion of a gas
quiz.answer: A reduction in density — the thinning out of a medium
quiz.explanation:
Rarefaction is a reduction in density — thinning out of a substance.
• rarefaction = a decrease in the density or pressure of something
• From Latin: 'rarus' (rare) + 'facere' (to make)
• 'Sound rarefaction', 'rarefied air' — reduced density

quiz.question: Olfaction is highly connected to memory in ways that other senses are not. What is olfaction?
options: The sense of smell — the ability to detect and identify odours
A sensory channel that triggers emotional memory more directly than others
The biological mechanism through which scent is detected
The faculty that connected experience to its stored emotional associations
quiz.answer: The sense of smell — the ability to detect and identify odours
quiz.explanation:
Olfaction is the sense of smell — the faculty for detecting odours.
• olfaction = the sense of smell
• From Latin: 'olfacere' — to smell
• 'Olfactory nerves', 'olfaction and memory' — the sense of smell

quiz.question: The interaction between the drug and alcohol produced dangerous side effects. What is interaction?
options: Reciprocal action — the way two or more things affect each other
A chemical reaction produced by combining two substances
An effect that could not have been predicted from either substance alone
A combination whose consequences required medical attention
quiz.answer: Reciprocal action — the way two or more things affect each other
quiz.explanation:
Interaction is reciprocal action — the mutual effect of two or more things.
• interaction = mutual or reciprocal action
• From Latin: 'inter-' + 'agere' (to act)
• 'Drug interaction', 'social interaction' — mutual effect

quiz.question: Only a fraction of the budget had been spent by the end of the year. What is a fraction?
options: A small part of a whole — or a number representing part of a unit
A proportion too small to have made a significant impact
The portion of funds that remained after the year's commitments, as the evidence showed
A share smaller than anyone had expected given the workload
quiz.answer: A small part of a whole — or a number representing part of a unit
quiz.explanation:
A fraction is a part of a whole — mathematically or figuratively.
• fraction = a numerical quantity less than a whole; a small part
• From Latin: 'frangere' — to break
• 'A fraction of the cost', 'vulgar fraction' — part of a whole

quiz.question: Refraction bends light when it passes from air into water. What is refraction?
options: The bending of a wave as it passes between media of different densities
The change of direction that light undergoes at a boundary
The optical phenomenon responsible for the bending of light in water, as the evidence showed
The process that explains why objects in water appear displaced
quiz.answer: The bending of a wave as it passes between media of different densities
quiz.explanation:
Refraction is the bending of waves — at the boundary between different media.
• refraction = the bending of waves passing from one medium to another
• From Latin: 'refringere' — to break up
• 'Light refraction', 'refractive index' — bending at a boundary

quiz.question: Minor infractions of the rules were dealt with informally. What is an infraction?
options: A violation of a rule or law — a breach
A failure to comply with the established standards
An act that fell short of what the rules required
A departure from procedure too minor to justify formal action
quiz.answer: A violation of a rule or law — a breach
quiz.explanation:
An infraction is a violation — a breach of a rule or law.
• infraction = a violation or breach of a rule
• From Latin: 'infringere' — to break
• 'Minor infraction', 'traffic infraction' — breaking a rule

quiz.question: Her occasional bluntness was a detraction from an otherwise impressive performance. What is detraction?
options: Something that takes away from the merit of something — a drawback
A quality that reduced the overall impression she made
A single fault that stood out against a background of strength
A minor weakness that reviewers noted despite the overall quality, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Something that takes away from the merit of something — a drawback
quiz.explanation:
A detraction is something that diminishes — taking away from value or reputation.
• detraction = the act of disparaging; something that detracts
• From Latin: 'detrahere' — to pull away
• 'A detraction from the achievement', 'detract from the quality' — reducing merit

quiz.question: She had a deep affection for the city she had grown up in. What is affection?
options: A feeling of fondness — gentle love or liking
The attachment that comes from shared history and memory
A warmth that had grown through a lifetime's experience of a place
The kind of love that required no occasion and demanded nothing
quiz.answer: A feeling of fondness — gentle love or liking
quiz.explanation:
Affection is fondness — a gentle, warm feeling of love or liking.
• affection = a feeling of fondness
• From Latin: 'affectio' — an inclination
• 'Show affection', 'terms of affection' — warm, fond feelings

quiz.question: The wound became infected because it had not been properly cleaned. What is infection?
options: The invasion and multiplication of microorganisms in body tissue
The consequence of inadequate wound care
A medical complication requiring antibiotic treatment
The process by which bacteria entered and damaged body tissue, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The invasion and multiplication of microorganisms in body tissue
quiz.explanation:
An infection is microbial invasion — microorganisms entering and multiplying in tissue.
• infection = the invasion of the body by pathogens
• From Latin: 'inficere' — to contaminate
• 'Bacterial infection', 'infected wound' — pathogen invasion

quiz.question: The doctor administered the injection quickly and painlessly. What is an injection?
options: The introduction of a substance into the body using a needle
A medical procedure that delivered medication directly into tissue
A clinical technique that bypassed the digestive system
A method of administration that ensured rapid absorption
quiz.answer: The introduction of a substance into the body using a needle
quiz.explanation:
An injection is the introduction of a substance — into the body by a needle.
• injection = the forcing of a liquid into something using a needle
• From Latin: 'injicere' — to throw in
• 'Administer an injection', 'cash injection' — forcing liquid in

quiz.question: The projection showed profits growing by fifteen percent over three years. What is a projection?
options: An estimate of future trends — a forecast based on current data
A calculation that extended present performance into the future, as the evidence showed
A financial forecast produced to support a funding application
A modelled outcome that assumed current conditions continued
quiz.answer: An estimate of future trends — a forecast based on current data
quiz.explanation:
A projection is a forecast — an estimate of future trends from current data.
• projection = an estimate of future trends; the act of projecting
• From Latin: 'projicere' — to throw forward
• 'Sales projection', 'population projection' — forecast from current data

quiz.question: His interjection of 'Nonsense!' brought the presentation to a halt. What is an interjection?
options: A sudden exclamation — an interruption inserted abruptly
An unsolicited comment that disrupted the flow of proceedings
A reaction that communicated his opinion before he had formulated it
An intervention that changed the atmosphere of the room
quiz.answer: A sudden exclamation — an interruption inserted abruptly
quiz.explanation:
An interjection is a sudden exclamation — an abrupt interruption.
• interjection = an exclamation; a word expressing emotion inserted into a sentence
• From Latin: 'interjicere' — to throw between
• 'Interjection in grammar', 'an interjection of surprise' — sudden exclamation

quiz.question: She had a predilection for detective fiction that she had never managed to explain. What is a predilection?
options: A preference — a particular liking for something
A taste formed early that had proved impossible to outgrow
A form of pleasure that required no justification to those who shared it
A personal taste that survived contact with more serious alternatives
quiz.answer: A preference — a particular liking for something
quiz.explanation:
A predilection is a preference — a particular fondness or liking.
• predilection = a preference; a tendency to like something
• From Latin: 'praediligere' — to prefer
• 'A predilection for jazz', 'predilection toward caution' — a preference

quiz.question: The museum's collection of Renaissance paintings was world-renowned. What is a collection?
options: A group of things gathered together — an assembled body of items
The sum of what an institution had acquired over many years
A body of objects whose combined significance exceeded any individual piece
An assembled group of works representing a period or tradition
quiz.answer: A group of things gathered together — an assembled body of items
quiz.explanation:
A collection is a gathered group — items assembled together.
• collection = a group of things gathered together
• From Latin: 'colligere' — to gather
• 'Art collection', 'debt collection', 'collector' — gathered together

quiz.question: The safety inspection revealed three critical faults. What is an inspection?
options: A careful examination — a formal check of something's condition
A process designed to identify problems before they caused harm
An official exercise that assessed compliance with required standards
A review conducted by qualified inspectors with authority to act
quiz.answer: A careful examination — a formal check of something's condition
quiz.explanation:
An inspection is a careful examination — a formal checking of something.
• inspection = careful examination; a formal review
• From Latin: 'inspicere' — to look at
• 'Safety inspection', 'inspection regime' — formal examination

quiz.question: The insurrection was put down within a week by the army. What is an insurrection?
options: A violent uprising against authority — a rebellion
An armed challenge to the legitimacy of the existing government
A military event requiring the deployment of regular forces
An attempt to seize power through organised violence
quiz.answer: A violent uprising against authority — a rebellion
quiz.explanation:
An insurrection is a violent uprising — a rebellion against authority.
• insurrection = a violent uprising against authority
• From Latin: 'insurgere' — to rise against
• 'Armed insurrection', 'suppress an insurrection' — violent revolt

quiz.question: The thorough dissection of the argument revealed its central flaw. What is dissection?
options: The detailed analysis of something — examining each part carefully
A critical technique that exposed what was concealed in the structure
A form of intellectual scrutiny that left nowhere for a weak argument to hide
The process of examining an argument part by part to find its weakness
quiz.answer: The detailed analysis of something — examining each part carefully
quiz.explanation:
Dissection is detailed analysis — taking something apart to examine it thoroughly.
• dissection = detailed analysis; cutting apart
• From Latin: 'dissecare' — to cut apart
• 'Critical dissection', 'anatomical dissection' — taking apart to examine

quiz.question: Early detection of cancer significantly improves survival rates. What is detection?
options: The discovery of something — finding what was hidden or not obvious
The identification of a condition before it had progressed significantly
The clinical process that preceded diagnosis and treatment
The point in a disease's progress when it became visible to medicine
quiz.answer: The discovery of something — finding what was hidden or not obvious
quiz.explanation:
Detection is discovery — finding something that was hidden or not easily seen.
• detection = the action of detecting; discovery
• From Latin: 'detegere' — to uncover
• 'Early detection', 'fraud detection' — finding the hidden

quiz.question: The old man left the village with a malediction on the lips of those who drove him out. What is a malediction?
options: A curse — a pronouncement of evil upon someone
A solemn statement that called misfortune down on those who had wronged him
A form of language associated with the most extreme expressions of anger
A verbal act that had force because of the moral authority behind it
quiz.answer: A curse — a pronouncement of evil upon someone
quiz.explanation:
A malediction is a curse — a formal utterance calling evil upon someone.
• malediction = a curse; the utterance of a curse
• From Latin: 'male' (bad) + 'dicere' (to speak)
• Opposite of benediction; 'utter a malediction' — calling down evil

quiz.question: The professor's valediction at the end of the final lecture moved many to tears. What is a valediction?
options: A farewell — words of parting spoken on a formal occasion
The formal conclusion to a course or relationship
A speech whose purpose was to bring something to a dignified close
Words chosen to mark an ending in a way that honoured what had come before
quiz.answer: A farewell — words of parting spoken on a formal occasion
quiz.explanation:
A valediction is a farewell — a formal address given at parting.
• valediction = a farewell; words spoken at parting
• From Latin: 'vale' (farewell) + 'dicere' (to say)
• 'Valedictory address', 'a valediction' — formal words of farewell

quiz.question: The ceremony closed with a benediction from the bishop. What is a benediction?
options: A blessing — words of divine favour pronounced at a ceremony
The formal religious conclusion to a service or gathering
A prayer that invoked God's protection on those present
The liturgical act that marked the end of a public religious ceremony
quiz.answer: A blessing — words of divine favour pronounced at a ceremony
quiz.explanation:
A benediction is a blessing — a prayer or pronouncement of divine favour.
• benediction = a spoken blessing; a closing prayer
• From Latin: 'bene' (well) + 'dicere' (to speak)
• Opposite of malediction; 'pronounce a benediction' — a blessing

quiz.question: The case fell outside the court's jurisdiction. What is jurisdiction?
options: The area or matter over which legal authority extends
The limits of what a court was empowered to decide
The legal territory within which a court's decisions had force
The formal boundary defining what was and was not within the court's power
quiz.answer: The area or matter over which legal authority extends
quiz.explanation:
Jurisdiction is the scope of legal authority — where a court's power applies.
• jurisdiction = the authority to make legal decisions
• From Latin: 'juris' (law) + 'dictio' (saying)
• 'Within jurisdiction', 'across jurisdictions' — where legal authority applies

quiz.question: The abandoned factory stood in dereliction at the edge of town. What is dereliction?
options: A state of disuse and abandonment — neglect and decay
The physical consequence of ownership that had ceased to care
The condition of a building left without maintenance or occupation
A visible reminder of an industry that had left decades earlier
quiz.answer: A state of disuse and abandonment — neglect and decay
quiz.explanation:
Dereliction is abandonment — neglected decay and disuse.
• dereliction = the state of being abandoned; neglect of duty
• From Latin: 'derelictus' — abandoned
• 'Dereliction of duty', 'buildings in dereliction' — abandonment and decay

quiz.question: The infliction of unnecessary suffering on animals is a criminal offence. What is infliction?
options: The act of causing harm — imposing something unpleasant on another
The active causing of pain prohibited by animal welfare legislation
A form of cruelty that the law recognised as requiring prosecution
The deliberate imposition of suffering on a being unable to resist
quiz.answer: The act of causing harm — imposing something unpleasant on another
quiz.explanation:
Infliction is the act of imposing — causing suffering or punishment on another.
• infliction = the act of inflicting
• From Latin: 'infligere' — to strike against
• 'Infliction of pain', 'inflict a wound' — causing something unpleasant

quiz.question: There was growing friction between the two departments over resources. What is friction?
options: Conflict or tension — resistance between opposing parties
A level of disagreement that was beginning to affect cooperation
The kind of tension that built slowly and was difficult to resolve
A professional relationship that had become more adversarial over time
quiz.answer: Conflict or tension — resistance between opposing parties
quiz.explanation:
Friction is resistance or conflict — the tension between opposing forces or parties.
• friction = the resistance of surfaces; conflict between parties
• From Latin: 'fricare' — to rub
• 'Create friction', 'physical friction' — opposing resistance

quiz.question: The family faced eviction after falling behind on rent. What is eviction?
options: The legal removal of a tenant from a property
A court-sanctioned action that ended a tenancy
The process by which a landlord recovered possession of a property
The removal carried out after legal process had been completed
quiz.answer: The legal removal of a tenant from a property
quiz.explanation:
Eviction is legal removal — expelling a tenant by legal process.
• eviction = the removal of a tenant by legal process
• From Latin: 'evincere' — to overcome
• 'Face eviction', 'eviction notice' — removal from property

quiz.question: She spoke with conviction — every sentence made clear she believed what she was saying. What is conviction?
options: A firm belief — the quality of being certain and sincere
The communication of genuine belief through confident expression
The kind of certainty that made her arguments difficult to dismiss
The strength of personal commitment that shaped how she expressed herself
quiz.answer: A firm belief — the quality of being certain and sincere
quiz.explanation:
Conviction is firm belief — the quality of being sincerely certain.
• conviction = a firmly held belief; a legal finding of guilt
• From Latin: 'convincere' — to overcome
• 'Speak with conviction', 'criminal conviction' — belief or guilty verdict

quiz.question: The committee gave its sanction for the new policy to proceed. What is a sanction?
options: Official approval or permission — also a penalty for violation
The formal authorisation required before the policy could be implemented
A decision by the appropriate body that the proposal was acceptable
The approval that moved the policy from proposal to implementation
quiz.answer: Official approval or permission — also a penalty for violation
quiz.explanation:
A sanction is approval — or a penalty; the word carries both meanings.
• sanction = official permission; a threatened penalty for non-compliance
• From Latin: 'sancire' — to make sacred
• 'Give sanction to', 'impose sanctions' — both approval and penalty

quiz.question: She graduated with distinction, the highest grade available. What is distinction?
options: Excellence that sets someone apart — a mark of superiority
The academic recognition of exceptional performance
A grade awarded only to those who exceeded the standard pass
A formal recognition of achievement at the highest level
quiz.answer: Excellence that sets someone apart — a mark of superiority
quiz.explanation:
Distinction is excellence — the quality of standing out above others.
• distinction = excellence; a difference; a mark of honour
• From Latin: 'distinguere' — to separate
• 'A mark of distinction', 'graduate with distinction' — outstanding quality

quiz.question: A malfunction in the cooling system caused the reactor to shut down. What is a malfunction?
options: A failure to function correctly — a fault in operation
A technical failure that triggered the automatic safety system
A fault serious enough to prevent normal operation
An engineering problem requiring immediate diagnosis and repair
quiz.answer: A failure to function correctly — a fault in operation
quiz.explanation:
A malfunction is a failure — a fault that prevents correct operation.
• malfunction = a failure to function correctly
• From Latin: 'male' (bad) + 'functio' (performance)
• 'Equipment malfunction', 'system malfunction' — operational failure

quiz.question: Turn left at the next junction and follow the road for two miles. What is a junction?
options: A point where two or more things meet — an intersection
A road intersection where the driver had to make a decision
The point at which one route diverged from another
A location on the road network requiring navigational attention
quiz.answer: A point where two or more things meet — an intersection
quiz.explanation:
A junction is a meeting point — where roads, wires, or paths come together.
• junction = a point where things meet or join
• From Latin: 'jungere' — to join
• 'Road junction', 'junction box' — a meeting or joining point

quiz.question: The court issued an injunction preventing publication of the article. What is an injunction?
options: A court order requiring someone to do or stop doing something
A legal instrument that prevented the publication from proceeding
An order whose breach would constitute contempt of court
A formal judicial command with immediate and enforceable effect
quiz.answer: A court order requiring someone to do or stop doing something
quiz.explanation:
An injunction is a court order — commanding or prohibiting an action.
• injunction = a judicial order requiring or preventing an action
• From Latin: 'injungere' — to impose upon
• 'Seek an injunction', 'court injunction' — a binding court order

quiz.question: Events occurred in conjunction — each made the other worse. What does 'in conjunction' mean?
options: Together — occurring at the same time and combining in effect
Simultaneously, in a way that compounded the impact of each
In combination, so that neither could be understood in isolation
At the same moment, with effects that fed into and amplified each other
quiz.answer: Together — occurring at the same time and combining in effect
quiz.explanation:
In conjunction means together — happening at the same time and combining.
• conjunction = union; a grammatical joining word; occurring together
• From Latin: 'conjungere' — to join
• 'In conjunction with', 'conjunctions in grammar' — joining or occurring together

quiz.question: There was a clear disjunction between what he promised and what he delivered. What is a disjunction?
options: A separation or disconnect — a gap between two things that should align
The contrast between stated intentions and observable behaviour, as the evidence showed
A discrepancy that those affected found impossible to overlook
The space between what was said and what was done
quiz.answer: A separation or disconnect — a gap between two things that should align
quiz.explanation:
A disjunction is a disconnect — a separation between things that should align.
• disjunction = a separation; a disconnect between things
• From Latin: 'disjungere' — to unyoke
• 'Cognitive disjunction', 'disjunction between theory and practice' — disconnect

quiz.question: She felt no compunction about exposing the fraud, however uncomfortable it made her. What is compunction?
options: A feeling of guilt or unease about an action
A moral discomfort that might have caused a less determined person to stop
The hesitation produced by awareness of potential consequences
The kind of scruple that some people felt and others did not
quiz.answer: A feeling of guilt or unease about an action
quiz.explanation:
Compunction is guilt or scruple — an uneasy feeling about a course of action.
• compunction = a feeling of guilt or moral scruple
• From Latin: 'compungere' — to prick sharply
• 'Without compunction', 'feel compunction' — guilt or moral hesitation

quiz.question: All new staff attended an induction programme during their first week. What is induction?
options: The process of formally introducing someone to a new role or environment
A structured introduction to the organisation's systems and culture
The first stage of employment in which expectations were established
A programme that reduced the time before a new employee could be productive
quiz.answer: The process of formally introducing someone to a new role or environment
quiz.explanation:
Induction is formal introduction — bringing someone into a new role or organisation.
• induction = the introduction of someone to a new post or role; logical inference
• From Latin: 'inducere' — to lead in
• 'Staff induction', 'induction programme', 'inductive reasoning' — leading in

quiz.question: The obstruction of justice is a serious criminal offence. What is obstruction?
options: The act of blocking or hindering — preventing something from proceeding
A deliberate interference with a legal process
An attempt to prevent the authorities from doing their job, as the evidence showed
An act whose effect was to prevent justice from being done
quiz.answer: The act of blocking or hindering — preventing something from proceeding
quiz.explanation:
Obstruction is blocking — preventing movement or action.
• obstruction = the act of blocking or hindering
• From Latin: 'obstruere' — to build against
• 'Obstruction of justice', 'road obstruction' — blocking

quiz.question: The accretion of small advantages eventually produced a decisive lead. What is accretion?
options: Growth through gradual accumulation — a build-up over time
The slow addition of advantages that compounded over many rounds
A process by which small gains became a significant total
The accumulation that was not noticeable in any single step but impressive in sum
quiz.answer: Growth through gradual accumulation — a build-up over time
quiz.explanation:
Accretion is gradual accumulation — growth through the addition of small amounts.
• accretion = growth by gradual accumulation
• From Latin: 'accrescere' — to grow
• 'Accretion of power', 'accretion disk' — gradual accumulation

quiz.question: The matter was handled with complete discretion — no one outside the room knew. What is discretion?
options: Careful judgment about what to reveal — keeping confidences
The professional quality that prevented sensitive matters from leaking
A standard of conduct that required judgment as well as silence
The combination of trustworthiness and good sense that the situation required
quiz.answer: Careful judgment about what to reveal — keeping confidences
quiz.explanation:
Discretion is careful judgment — especially about what to reveal.
• discretion = the quality of behaving carefully; freedom to decide
• From Latin: 'discernere' — to separate
• 'Use discretion', 'at your discretion' — good judgment; freedom to decide

quiz.question: The new wing was a welcome addition to the hospital. What is an addition?
options: Something added — a supplementary element joined to an existing whole
An investment that increased the hospital's capacity to serve patients
A building that addressed an identified gap in the hospital's provision
A project that had been planned for years before funding was secured
quiz.answer: Something added — a supplementary element joined to an existing whole
quiz.explanation:
An addition is something added — a supplementary element or the act of adding.
• addition = something added; the process of adding
• From Latin: 'addere' — to add
• 'A new addition', 'in addition to' — something added

quiz.question: The pamphlet was prosecuted as sedition for inciting rebellion against the crown. What is sedition?
options: Speech or conduct that incites rebellion — subversive agitation
The publication of material intended to undermine the authority of the state
A form of expression prohibited by laws protecting established power
Writing designed to encourage readers to act against the government
quiz.answer: Speech or conduct that incites rebellion — subversive agitation
quiz.explanation:
Sedition is incitement to rebellion — speech or action against state authority.
• sedition = conduct or speech inciting rebellion
• From Latin: 'seditio' — going apart
• 'Charged with sedition', 'seditious libel' — inciting against authority

quiz.question: The coalition government survived for three years before collapsing. What is a coalition?
options: A union of separate parties — working together toward a common purpose
A political arrangement in which two parties shared government
A partnership of convenience that lasted until its usefulness expired
A formal agreement between parties who might otherwise have been opponents
quiz.answer: A union of separate parties — working together toward a common purpose
quiz.explanation:
A coalition is a union — separate parties coming together for a shared purpose.
• coalition = a union of separate parties for a common purpose
• From Latin: 'coalescere' — to grow together
• 'Coalition government', 'form a coalition' — joining together

quiz.question: The abolition of slavery was achieved only after prolonged struggle. What is abolition?
options: The formal ending of a practice — complete and official elimination
The legislative act that made a previously legal practice illegal
The culmination of a reform movement that had lasted generations
The legal transformation that turned an accepted practice into a crime
quiz.answer: The formal ending of a practice — complete and official elimination
quiz.explanation:
Abolition is the formal ending — the complete official elimination of a practice.
• abolition = the formal elimination of a practice or institution
• From Latin: 'abolere' — to destroy
• 'Abolition of slavery', 'abolitionist movement' — formal elimination

quiz.question: The demolition of the old factory took several weeks. What is demolition?
options: The act of tearing down — the destruction of a structure
The removal of a building no longer fit for purpose
A process requiring specialist equipment and safety precautions
The beginning of a redevelopment that would transform the site
quiz.answer: The act of tearing down — the destruction of a structure
quiz.explanation:
Demolition is the tearing down — the destruction of something built.
• demolition = the act of demolishing a building or structure
• From Latin: 'demoliri' — to pull down
• 'Demolition crew', 'demolition of arguments' — tearing down

quiz.question: She left of her own volition — no one pressured her into the decision. What is volition?
options: The exercise of will — acting through one's own free choice
A decision made without external pressure or obligation
A choice that reflected her own judgment rather than others' influence
An action taken because she wanted to rather than because she had to
quiz.answer: The exercise of will — acting through one's own free choice
quiz.explanation:
Volition is will — the exercise of one's own free choice.
• volition = the faculty or power of using one's will
• From Latin: 'volo' — I wish
• 'Of her own volition', 'act with volition' — freely willed choice

quiz.question: The army ran short of munitions after three weeks of heavy fighting. What are munitions?
options: Military weapons and ammunition — the materials of war
The supplies required for sustained combat operations
The shells, missiles, and bullets consumed in combat
The military stores that determined how long a campaign could be sustained
quiz.answer: Military weapons and ammunition — the materials of war
quiz.explanation:
Munitions are military supplies — weapons and ammunition for warfare.
• munition = military supplies; weapons and ammunition
• From Latin: 'munitio' — fortification
• 'Munitions factory', 'munitions dump' — weapons and ammunition

quiz.question: His contrition seemed genuine — he wept as he apologised. What is contrition?
options: Deep remorse for wrongdoing — genuine sorrow for a fault
An emotional state that accompanied an apology he needed to make
The kind of sorrow that required expression rather than suppression
A feeling whose visible signs supported the sincerity of his apology
quiz.answer: Deep remorse for wrongdoing — genuine sorrow for a fault
quiz.explanation:
Contrition is deep remorse — genuine sorrow for having done wrong.
• contrition = the state of feeling remorseful
• From Latin: 'conterere' — to grind down
• 'Acts of contrition', 'contrite apology' — heartfelt sorrow for wrongdoing

quiz.question: The campaign was one of attrition — wearing the enemy down through sustained pressure. What is attrition?
options: The gradual wearing down through sustained pressure or loss
A military strategy based on inflicting greater losses than one suffered
A form of conflict in which time and resources were the deciding factors
A process in which the stronger side waited for the other to exhaust itself
quiz.answer: The gradual wearing down through sustained pressure or loss
quiz.explanation:
Attrition is gradual wearing down — reducing strength through sustained loss.
• attrition = the process of wearing something down gradually
• From Latin: 'atterere' — to rub against
• 'War of attrition', 'staff attrition' — gradual wearing down

quiz.question: Good nutrition in early childhood has lifelong effects on health. What is nutrition?
options: The process of taking in food — the supply of nutrients for health
The dietary foundation on which later physical and cognitive development rests
The intake of substances that supported growth during a critical period
The component of health most influenced by poverty and access to food
quiz.answer: The process of taking in food — the supply of nutrients for health
quiz.explanation:
Nutrition is the process of nourishing — taking in food that provides nutrients.
• nutrition = the process of providing or receiving nutrients
• From Latin: 'nutrire' — to nourish
• 'Poor nutrition', 'nutritional value' — the supply and intake of food nutrients

quiz.question: Parturition was complicated by the position of the foal. What is parturition?
options: The process of giving birth
The final stage of pregnancy that required veterinary supervision
The physical event that concluded the gestation period
A biological process that occurred at the end of a pregnancy
quiz.answer: The process of giving birth
quiz.explanation:
Parturition is the act of giving birth — the process of delivery.
• parturition = the act of giving birth
• From Latin: 'parturire' — to be in labour
• 'Complicated parturition', 'parturient' — relating to giving birth

quiz.question: He launched into an extended disquisition on the history of Roman roads. What is a disquisition?
options: A long, formal discourse or investigation — a thorough examination
An extended discussion that went well beyond the immediate point
A spoken essay that drew on extensive research
A lengthy contribution to a conversation that others had to wait through
quiz.answer: A long, formal discourse or investigation — a thorough examination
quiz.explanation:
A disquisition is a detailed formal examination — a thorough discourse.
• disquisition = a long or elaborate essay or discussion
• From Latin: 'disquirere' — to investigate
• 'A lengthy disquisition', 'written disquisition' — formal extended discourse

quiz.question: The lawyer's deposition of the key witness took four hours. What is a deposition?
options: The formal sworn testimony of a witness — or the removal from power
A legal procedure that recorded witness evidence outside of court
A formal process allowing attorneys to question a witness in advance
A statement made under oath and transcribed for use in proceedings
quiz.answer: The formal sworn testimony of a witness — or the removal from power
quiz.explanation:
A deposition is sworn testimony — evidence given out of court; also a removal from power.
• deposition = sworn testimony; also the removal of a ruler from power
• From Latin: 'deponere' — to put down
• 'Take a deposition', 'the deposition of the king' — testimony or overthrow

quiz.question: In the sentence 'The book is on the table,' the word 'on' is a preposition. What is a preposition?
options: A word expressing the relationship between a noun and other elements
A function word that indicates location, direction, or time
A grammatical class connecting a noun or pronoun to the rest of a sentence
A part of speech that establishes the relationship between two nouns
quiz.answer: A word expressing the relationship between a noun and other elements
quiz.explanation:
A preposition is a relational word — expressing the connection between elements.
• preposition = a word placed before a noun to indicate relationship
• From Latin: 'praeponere' — to place before
• 'In', 'on', 'at', 'by' — prepositions

quiz.question: Decomposition of organic matter returns nutrients to the soil. What is decomposition?
options: The breaking down of matter — the decay of organic substances
The biological process by which dead material is returned to the ecosystem
A natural cycle that converted dead organic matter into soil nutrients
A microbial process essential to the functioning of ecosystems
quiz.answer: The breaking down of matter — the decay of organic substances
quiz.explanation:
Decomposition is breakdown — the decay and disintegration of organic matter.
• decomposition = the process of breaking down into simpler elements
• From Latin: 'de-' + 'componere' (to put together)
• 'Organic decomposition', 'mathematical decomposition' — breaking down

quiz.question: The proposition that all humans are equal is a cornerstone of democratic thought. What is a proposition?
options: A statement put forward for consideration — a claim or proposal
A foundational claim on which a political philosophy rested, as the evidence showed
A statement simple enough to be contested or defended
An assertion that could be either accepted or rejected
quiz.answer: A statement put forward for consideration — a claim or proposal
quiz.explanation:
A proposition is a statement — put forward for acceptance or discussion.
• proposition = a statement or proposal put forward for consideration
• From Latin: 'proponere' — to propose
• 'A proposition of logic', 'business proposition' — a claim or offer

quiz.question: The question 'Have you stopped lying?' contains a presupposition. What is a presupposition?
options: An assumption embedded in a statement — taken for granted without proof
A premise that must be accepted before the question makes sense
Something the questioner assumed was true without saying so
An assumption loaded into a question that could not be denied without difficulty
quiz.answer: An assumption embedded in a statement — taken for granted without proof
quiz.explanation:
A presupposition is an embedded assumption — something taken for granted.
• presupposition = an assumption implicit in a statement
• From Latin: 'prae-' + 'supponere' (to suppose beforehand)
• 'The presupposition behind the question' — an unspoken assumed premise

quiz.question: She had a cheerful disposition that made her popular with colleagues. What is a disposition?
options: A person's temperament — their characteristic way of thinking or feeling
A natural quality that expressed itself consistently across situations
The underlying personality trait that shaped how she related to others
A character feature that distinguished her from those with different natures
quiz.answer: A person's temperament — their characteristic way of thinking or feeling
quiz.explanation:
A disposition is temperament — a person's characteristic inclinations or tendencies.
• disposition = a person's inherent qualities; a tendency
• From Latin: 'disponere' — to arrange
• 'A cheerful disposition', 'disposition toward caution' — natural tendency

quiz.question: A genetic predisposition to the condition does not mean it will definitely develop. What is a predisposition?
options: A tendency or susceptibility — inclination toward something in advance
A genetic or environmental factor that increased the probability of a condition
A background risk factor whose effect depended on other variables
An inherited characteristic that made an outcome more likely
quiz.answer: A tendency or susceptibility — inclination toward something in advance
quiz.explanation:
A predisposition is a prior tendency — an inclination toward something before it occurs.
• predisposition = a liability or tendency toward something
• From Latin: 'prae-' + 'disponere'
• 'Genetic predisposition', 'predisposition to anxiety' — prior tendency

quiz.question: The exposition at the beginning of the play established the setting and characters. What is exposition?
options: Background information that establishes the context of a work
The opening section in which essential information was provided
The part of a narrative that introduced the world before the action began
A technique used to give the audience what they needed before events unfolded
quiz.answer: Background information that establishes the context of a work
quiz.explanation:
Exposition is background information — establishing context at the start of a work.
• exposition = an explanation of a subject; background in a narrative
• From Latin: 'exponere' — to set out
• 'Narrative exposition', 'art exposition' — setting out information

quiz.question: The partition of India in 1947 created two new nations. What is a partition?
options: The division of a country or space into separate parts
The political event that created two independent states from one
The geographical and administrative division that accompanied independence
The act of drawing a boundary that transformed political reality
quiz.answer: The division of a country or space into separate parts
quiz.explanation:
A partition is a division — splitting into separate parts.
• partition = the division of something into parts
• From Latin: 'partire' — to divide
• 'Partition of India', 'room partition' — dividing into parts

quiz.question: Breaking a mirror is an example of a superstition that many dismiss but few entirely ignore. What is a superstition?
options: A belief based on fear or ignorance rather than reason or evidence
A traditional belief that associated an act with an unrelated consequence
A form of magical thinking that survived in the absence of rational alternatives
A belief whose persistence revealed the limits of rationalism in daily life
quiz.answer: A belief based on fear or ignorance rather than reason or evidence
quiz.explanation:
A superstition is an irrational belief — unfounded, usually involving fear of the unknown.
• superstition = a belief not based on reason or knowledge
• From Latin: 'superstare' — to stand over
• 'Old superstition', 'superstitious fear' — irrational belief

quiz.question: After ten years, the plan finally came to fruition. What is fruition?
options: The point at which a plan or effort bears results — realisation
The moment at which years of work produced their intended outcome
The achievement that justified everything that had preceded it
The stage at which a long-term plan delivered what had been promised
quiz.answer: The point at which a plan or effort bears results — realisation
quiz.explanation:
Fruition is realisation — the point at which efforts produce their desired result.
• fruition = the realisation of a plan or project
• From Latin: 'frui' — to enjoy
• 'Come to fruition', 'bring to fruition' — producing the desired result

quiz.question: Her intuition told her something was wrong before she could articulate why. What is intuition?
options: The ability to know or sense something without conscious reasoning
A form of knowledge that preceded and outran logical explanation
A signal produced by experience that bypassed conscious analysis
The kind of awareness that proved correct without being able to explain itself
quiz.answer: The ability to know or sense something without conscious reasoning
quiz.explanation:
Intuition is instinctive knowledge — understanding without conscious analysis.
• intuition = the ability to understand without conscious reasoning
• From Latin: 'intueri' — to look at
• 'Trust your intuition', 'intuitive leap' — knowing without reasoning
